overall_summary: Eversource supports clarity for large-load interconnection but urges the Commission to keep any final rule flexible rather than prescriptive, warning that a one-size-fits-all process would impede rather than facilitate interconnection. It asks FERC to preserve existing and future retail transmission-voltage arrangements, which large loads overwhelmingly prefer, and to make participation in the Order No. 2023 generator cluster study process optional rather than mandatory for loads under time pressure.

quotes.text: an overly prescriptive and restrictive final rule arising out of this proceeding could result in more harm than the status quo to prospective large load customers, interconnecting transmission owners, and the public generally.
quotes.concern: A prescriptive final rule could do more harm than the status quo.

quotes.text: implementing a strict, one-size-fits-all interconnection process will impede rather than facilitate the interconnection of these large loads.
quotes.concern: A uniform process would impede large-load interconnection.

quotes.text: the Commission should also ensure that any final rule provides sufficient flexibility to address the different needs of large-load customers.
quotes.concern: Final rule needs flexibility for varied load needs.

quotes.text: Any final rule arising out of this proceeding must be flexible enough to accommodate different load types and their needs.
quotes.concern: Rule must accommodate diverse load types.

quotes.text: the final rule should also be flexible enough to encompass different “hybrid” load arrangements, including those co-located with generation seeking standby service, behind-the-meter generation with voluntary curtailment/isolation, and other arrangements that may be sought by customers.
quotes.concern: Rule should cover hybrid and co-located load arrangements.

quotes.text: The final rule should also be flexible enough to allow transmission providers to develop and address region-specific rules and concerns (such as termination of co-located generator interconnection rights).
quotes.concern: Allow region-specific rules including co-located rights termination.

quotes.text: the Commission should preserve both existing and prospective retail interconnections at transmission voltages in order to efficiently interconnect large loads to the system.
In Eversource’s experience, prospective large loads overwhelmingly prefer the retail construct.
quotes.concern: Large loads prefer the state retail construct.

quotes.text: the Commission could consider a voltage or MW threshold (likely much greater than 20 MW) such that transmission providers could still require the largest loads to participate in a federal interconnection process.
quotes.concern: A threshold well above 20 MW could trigger federal process.

quotes.text: any specific threshold may result in loads artificially reducing their capacity to just below that threshold to avoid such requirements, which could result in greater inefficiencies overall.
quotes.concern: Thresholds invite gaming that reduces capacity below the line.

quotes.text: to the extent any final rule arising out of this proceeding contemplates including qualifying loads in the Order No. 2023-mandated cluster study process, such inclusion should be optional.
quotes.concern: Inclusion in the cluster study process should be optional.

quotes.text: the strict and often lengthy study timelines for generator interconnection may not be practically feasible with the tight timelines for the interconnection of large loads
quotes.concern: Generator study timelines are too slow for time-pressured loads.

quotes.text: the Commission should consider not including loads in that process at all due to the time pressures described above.
quotes.concern: Loads could be excluded from the cluster study process entirely.

quotes.text: The Commission could also create an expedited study process for all large loads under significant time pressure, or allow those loads to avail themselves of state retail processes.
quotes.concern: Create an expedited study process for time-pressured loads.

quotes.text: none of its current or prospective large load customers have expressed any interest in mandatory/dispatchable curtailment.
quotes.concern: Customers show no interest in mandatory dispatchable curtailment.

bins.name: Flexible, not prescriptive rule
bins.description: Eversource argues a strict one-size-fits-all interconnection process would impede rather than facilitate large-load interconnection and could cause more harm than the status quo. It urges sufficient flexibility to address varied load types, hybrid arrangements, and region-specific rules.

bins.name: Flexibility for hybrid arrangements
bins.description: The filer wants the rule flexible enough to cover hybrid load arrangements, including co-located generation with standby service and behind-the-meter generation with voluntary curtailment, and to let transmission providers address region-specific concerns such as termination of co-located generator interconnection rights.

bins.name: Co-located load arrangements
bins.description: Eversource asks the final rule to accommodate loads co-located with generation and to let transmission providers set region-specific rules governing co-located generator interconnection rights.

bins.name: Preserve state retail arrangements
bins.description: Eversource asks the Commission to preserve existing and prospective retail interconnections at transmission voltages, noting that prospective large loads overwhelmingly prefer the state-jurisdictional retail construct over the federal process.

bins.name: MW threshold above 20 MW
bins.description: The filer suggests a voltage or MW threshold well above 20 MW could let transmission providers require only the largest loads to use a federal process, but cautions that any fixed threshold invites loads to artificially reduce capacity below the line, causing greater inefficiency.

bins.name: Optional cluster study for loads
bins.description: Eversource opposes mandatory inclusion of loads in the Order No. 2023 cluster study process, arguing its strict and lengthy timelines are infeasible for time-pressured large loads. Inclusion should be optional, and the Commission could consider not including loads at all.

bins.name: No mandatory joint load-generator study
bins.description: The filer resists studying loads together with generators on a mandatory basis, because generator study timelines cannot meet the tight schedules large loads face. Any inclusion in the joint cluster process should be optional or avoided entirely.

bins.name: Expedited process, voluntary curtailment
bins.description: Eversource supports an expedited study process for large loads under time pressure or access to state retail processes. It reports its customers show no interest in mandatory dispatchable curtailment, suggesting eligibility be tied to voluntary curtailment instead.
